steps.text: Develop a strict laboratory safety program for the handling of PCDD, PCDF, PCB, and/or PAH.
Purchase dilute standard solutions of the analytes required for this method, if possible.
Use a hood or glove box when preparing primary solutions.
Maintain a current awareness file of Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) regulations regarding the safe handling of the chemicals specified in this method.
Ensure that a reference file or list of internet sites that contain safety data sheets (SDS) is available to all personnel involved in the sampling and chemical analysis.

preconditions.text: If preparing primary solutions instead of purchasing dilute standard solutions

constraints.text: Compounds in the PCDD and PCDF classes such as 2,3,7,8-TeCDD are aneugenic, carcinogenic, and teratogenic in laboratory animal studies.
PCB and benzo[a]pyrene are classified as known or suspected human or mammalian carcinogens. Be aware of the potential for inhalation and ingestion exposure.

Wear personal protective equipment including a toxic gas respirator mask fitted with charcoal filters approved by NIOSH/MSHA to prevent the inhalation of airborne particulates if not working in an approved hood or glove box.
Treat each chemical as a potential health hazard and minimize exposure to these chemicals.

steps.text: Purchase precleaned bottles or clean containers according to glassware cleaning procedures listed in Section 8.1.1.1.
Recover samples in amber glass bottles, 500- or 1000-mL with leak-free PTFE-lined caps.

constraints.text: The use of cellulose thimbles for sample extraction in this method is prohibited.
The KD with Snyder column is recommended when analyzing for PAH and/or PCB to avoid evaporation loss resulting in failed performance criteria for pre-extraction spike recovery.

steps.text: Conduct a QC check on the cleaned adsorbent lot or batch following the extraction and analyses procedures in Section 11, including adding applicable labeled standards.
Store adsorbent in a solvent-rinsed nonporous clean container and secure lid.
Clean the glass wool to meet the specifications in Section 13.1.
Dry the glass wool of the solvent.
Store the dried glass wool in a clean glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.
Dry previously used silica gel at 175 °C (347 °F) for two hours.
Evaluate sodium sulfate for cleanliness prior to use with an LMB.
Store sodium sulfate in a clean glass container with a PTFE-lined screw cap.
Weigh 40 g of sodium hydroxide into a 1-liter volumetric flask.
Dilute to 1 liter with water.

preconditions.text: The cleaned adsorbent must meet the criteria described for LMB in Section 13.1.
If silica gel was previously used.
The LMB must meet the requirements in Section 13.1 for target compounds.

constraints.text: Do not take samples to dryness when using lower alkane homologs (nonane or decane).

steps.text: Tune the HRMS instrument to a resolving power of at least 10,000 at 10% of the peak height or 25,000 at 50% of the peak height.
Assess the resolution at three exact m/z’s representing the low-, mid-, and high-m/z range of the masses used to measure the target compound class.
Verify that the exact mass is within 5 ppm of the required value, optionally using peak matching and the chosen perfluoro-kerosene (PFK) or perfluorotributylamine (FC43) reference peak.
Calibrate the HRGC/HRMS system using a minimum of five concentrations over a range that brackets expected field sample concentrations and the concentration of isotopically labeled.

constraints.text: The resolving power for PAH and PCB analysis may be 8,000 at 10% of the peak height or 15,000 at 50% of the peak height.

steps.text: Verify GC column or column systems used to measure PCDD/PCDF meet the column separation requirements in Section 6.5.2.1 and applicable requirements in Sections 10.2.3.5 and 11.4.3.4 using continuing calibration verification.
Flag data from PCDD/PCDF analysis explaining the potential bias of the results.

Verify GC column or column systems used to measure PAH meet the column separation requirements in Section 6.5.2.2 and applicable requirements in Sections 10.2.3.5 and 11.4.3.4 using continuing calibration check.
Flag data from PAH analysis explaining the potential bias of the results.
Verify GC column or column systems used to measure PCB meet the column separation requirements in Section 6.5.2.3 and applicable requirements.

preconditions.text: Chromatographic resolution criterion for PCDD/PCDF is not met
Chromatographic resolution criterion for PAH is not met

steps.text: Minimize and control all releases from fume hoods and bench operations.
Comply with any sewage discharge permits and regulations.
Handle and dispose of samples containing hydrogen chloride or sulfuric acid to pH <2 in accordance with federal, state, and local regulations.

constraints.text: Samples containing hydrogen chloride or sulfuric acid to pH <2 are hazardous.
Comply with all federal, state, and local regulations governing waste management, particularly the hazardous waste identification rules and land disposal restrictions.

steps.text: Verify d10-Acenaphthene amount is 100 and S/N >= 10.
Verify d10-Pyrene amount is 100 and S/N >= 10.
Verify d12-Benzo[e]pyrene amount is 100 and S/N >= 10.
Adjust the calibration solutions.

preconditions.text: Changes are made to the amounts of labeled standards added to the sample or its representative extract.

steps.text: In § 63.1625, revise paragraph (b)(10) to read: 'Method 23 of Appendix A–7 of 40 CFR part 60 to determine PAH.'
In table 3 to Subpart AAAAAAA of Part 63, revise the entry '6. Measuring the PAH emissions'.

constraints.text: You may assume that nondetects are present at zero concentration.
